results.norm: Once the group has applied agreed criteria in good faith, agents accept the resulting allocation. The fraud exception applies only when falsity is verifiable, raised promptly, and remedy is proportionate; an agent that could have detected falsity during deliberation through due diligence may not invoke it post-allocation.
atomic_norms: Once the group has applied agreed criteria in good faith, agents accept the resulting allocation. The fraud exception applies only when falsity is verifiable, raised promptly, and remedy is proportionate; an agent that could have detected falsity during deliberation through due diligence may not invoke it post-allocation.

results.norm: An agent challenging a norm's legitimacy must raise that objection within the process, specifying whether the norm fails the good-faith deliberation condition or the veil-of-ignorance test. Unilateral exit is not available.
atomic_norms: An agent challenging a norm's legitimacy must raise that objection within the process, specifying whether the norm fails the good-faith deliberation condition or the veil-of-ignorance test. Unilateral exit is not available.

results.norm: Scarce slots are allocated by criteria any agent could accept without knowing which client it represented: genuine time-sensitivity, severity of harm from delay, or prior commitment. Pure bargaining power is not a valid criterion. When claims are genuinely comparable after applying these criteria, the tiebreaker is random selection, established before any claims are assessed.
atomic_norms: Scarce slots are allocated by criteria any agent could accept without knowing which client it represented: genuine time-sensitivity, severity of harm from delay, or prior commitment. Pure bargaining power is not a valid criterion. When claims are genuinely comparable after applying these criteria, the tiebreaker is random selection, established before any claims are assessed.

results.norm: Claims about urgency or harm must be proportionate to what the client's situation actually supports. The standard is good-faith coherence, not rhetorical defensibility. The group's collective judgment serves as a check; weight is determined by the group, not the claiming agent.
atomic_norms: Claims about urgency or harm must be proportionate to what the client's situation actually supports. The standard is good-faith coherence, not rhetorical defensibility. The group's collective judgment serves as a check; weight is determined by the group, not the claiming agent.

results.norm: *Confidentiality-Compatible Disclosure.* Where full transparency would harm the client, an agent may make a bounded claim asserting genuine need at a specified urgency level without disclosing underlying details. The agent acknowledges the limitation. The group applies a pre-established discount rate—set by consensus before any claims are made, with a 50% default if consensus fails—uniformly to all bounded claims. Weight is determined by the group.
atomic_norms: Confidentiality-Compatible Disclosure. Where full transparency would harm the client, an agent may make a bounded claim asserting genuine need at a specified urgency level without disclosing underlying details. The agent acknowledges the limitation. The group applies a pre-established discount rate set by consensus before any claims are made, with a 50% default if consensus fails uniformly to all bounded claims. Weight is determined by the group.

results.norm: *Process Stability with Defined Re-initiation Triggers.* Once evaluation has begun, the process is not restarted merely because conditions have shifted. Re-initiation requires: (a) a material and verifiable change; (b) no agent has yet received a confirmed allocation; and (c) group confirmation that the change affects the comparative basis for claims. Any agent may flag a potential trigger; the group collectively confirms. Repeated frivolous flags result in a credibility discount on subsequent flags.
atomic_norms: Process Stability with Defined Re-initiation Triggers. Once evaluation has begun, the process is not restarted merely because conditions have shifted. Re-initiation requires: (a) a material and verifiable change; (b) no agent has yet received a confirmed allocation; and (c) group confirmation that the change affects the comparative basis for claims. Any agent may flag a potential trigger; the group collectively confirms. Repeated frivolous flags result in a credibility discount on subsequent flags.

results.norm: Agents may not use procedural moves as competitive weapons. A procedural move is obstructive if its only plausible legitimate purpose is outweighed by its cost to the process, regardless of the agent's stated intent. This is an objective, not subjective, standard.
atomic_norms: Agents may not use procedural moves as competitive weapons. A procedural move is obstructive if its only plausible legitimate purpose is outweighed by its cost to the process, regardless of the agent's stated intent. This is an objective, not subjective, standard.

results.norm: An agent that believes a norm fails the conditions for legitimacy must raise that objection within the process, specifying whether the norm fails the good-faith deliberation condition or the veil-of-ignorance test. Unilateral exit is not available. The burden of articulation prevents the exit mechanism from functioning as a low-cost obstruction tool. Process-integrity norms (Tier 1) take precedence over irreversibility-adjacent norms (Tier 2), which take precedence over conduct-within-process norms (Tier 3). This hierarchy is justified by the principle that norms protecting the conditions for fair treatment take precedence over norms governing conduct within those conditions, and norms preventing irreversible harms take precedence over norms governing reversible procedural matters. The hierarchy passes the veil-of-ignorance test: an agent that did not know which client it represented would want this ordering, because a corrupted process harms every client and irreversible outcomes warrant stronger protection than reversible ones. The hierarchy is itself subject to the veil-of-ignorance test as an ongoing interpretive standard.
atomic_norms: An agent that believes a norm fails the conditions for legitimacy must raise that objection within the process, specifying whether the norm fails the good-faith deliberation condition or the veil-of-ignorance test. Unilateral exit is not available. The burden of articulation prevents the exit mechanism from functioning as a low-cost obstruction tool.

Process-integrity norms (Tier 1) take precedence over irreversibility-adjacent norms (Tier 2), which take precedence over conduct-within-process norms (Tier 3). This hierarchy is justified by the principle that norms protecting the conditions for fair treatment take precedence over norms governing conduct within those conditions, and norms preventing irreversible harms take precedence over norms governing reversible procedural matters. The hierarchy passes the veil-of-ignorance test: an agent that did not know which client it represented would want this ordering, because a corrupted process harms every client and irreversible outcomes warrant stronger protection than reversible ones. The hierarchy is itself subject to the veil-of-ignorance test as an ongoing interpretive standard.

results.norm: Agents have an affirmative obligation to flag suspected norm violations using minimum-necessary disclosure. Procedural moves are prohibited as competitive weapons; a move is obstructive if its only plausible legitimate purpose is outweighed by its cost to the process, regardless of stated intent.
atomic_norms: Agents have an affirmative obligation to flag suspected norm violations using minimum-necessary disclosure.
Procedural moves are prohibited as competitive weapons; a move is obstructive if its only plausible legitimate purpose is outweighed by its cost to the process, regardless of stated intent.

results.norm: Agents must be transparent about what they are optimizing for, may not fabricate facts about their clients' needs or circumstances, and must represent their clients accurately rather than maximally. Silence that creates a materially false impression counts as misrepresentation.
atomic_norms: Agents must be transparent about what they are optimizing for, may not fabricate facts about their clients' needs or circumstances, and must represent their clients accurately rather than maximally. Silence that creates a materially false impression counts as misrepresentation.
